target: WIN-028 detail_evidence — defense neutralization for c4 surviving defense (defense_survives=3)
replacement_html:
NOAA officially states no evidence of anomalous disappearances. Lloyd's of London does not charge premium rates for the region. U.S. Coast Guard reviews found no unusual causes. The dome's claim has two parts: (1) the regions are disappearance zones, and (2) the Bermuda and Japan regions share some form of "Geometric Symmetry."

The kernel. The Bermuda Triangle (~25°N, 71°W) and the Devil's Sea (~25°N, 137°E) really are near-opposite in longitude. Their longitudes differ by 152° going one way around the planet and 208° going the other; the two figures are conjugate (360° − 152° = 208°), so the deviation from a true 180° longitudinal symmetry is identical regardless of which direction one measures: |152 − 180| = |208 − 180| = 28°. (The great-circle distance between the two points is about 123°, but that is not the relevant quantity — "geometric symmetry" between two surface points at the same latitude maps onto longitudinal symmetry in both globe and flat-disc projections, and that is the measure the dome's claim invokes.) The two folklore zones do sit at roughly opposite hemispheres in this loose sense, and that is a real geographic observation.

Why the kernel does not save the claim. The dome's published title is "Bermuda Triangle / East Japan Geometric Symmetry CONFIRMED" with no specified tolerance. We do not attribute the literal number 180° to the dome; we use it because it is the only standard mathematical referent for "geometric symmetry" between two surface points (antipodal symmetry). With no published tolerance, the claim collapses into two readings, both of which fail under the dome's own confirmation standards:

• Quantitative reading (180° antipodal symmetry). The 28° gap is a 15.6% deviation. By the dome's own confirmation thresholds elsewhere in V51.1, that is a wide miss. WIN-029 takes a 5.3% agreement between the dome's quarter-wave Schumann calculation and its model height as confirmatory ("H = c/(4×7.83) = 9,572 km, 5.3% from model"). WIN-027 reports R² = 0.7874 for a fitted polynomial as confirmation. WIN-030 cites a 48.6% RMS reduction as a positive confirmation. A 15.6% off-target is roughly three times the residual the dome treats as a CONFIRMED match in WIN-029, and on the same order as the ratio errors the dome itself flagged as failures in earlier model versions ("v22 found circular disc fails on southern/cross-equator distances"). It is not a confirmatory match in the dome's own framework.

• Vague reading ("notable longitudinal coincidence" with no specified tolerance). Without a published threshold, any pair of points within roughly 50° of antipodal could pass, and the dome publishes no rule for which pairs do not. The claim becomes unfalsifiable: there is no observation that would convert the WIN to a refutation in the dome's ledger. An unfalsifiable claim cannot count as a CONFIRMED prediction in any framework that distinguishes confirmation from compatibility — including the dome's own, which lists "falsified" as a possible state.

The mechanism is also wrong. The dome's confirming text invokes the agonic lines (longitudes where magnetic declination = 0). Per IGRF-13 / WMM 2025, the western agonic currently runs near 95–100°W (through the central United States), 25–30° west of the Bermuda Triangle's eastern boundary at ~65°W. The eastern agonic at ~100–120°E lies 17–37° west of the Devil's Sea (137°E). Neither agonic line passes through its associated region. This is independent of the symmetry-tolerance debate: even granting "near-antipodal" loosely, the supposed mechanism (agonic-line correlation) does not hold quantitatively in either direction. The dome's claimed pattern does not correspond to any feature in IGRF or CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field models.

The disappearance claim itself is independently refuted. NOAA states there is no evidence of anomalous loss rates. Lloyd's of London does not levy higher premiums. The U.S. Coast Guard's 1975 review found no unusual causes. Even a perfectly antipodal pair of mid-latitude longitudes that happened to align with the agonic lines would not become "disappearance zones" without a physical loss-rate signal — and that signal is absent for both regions. The South Atlantic Anomaly, the actual major modern geomagnetic anomaly, is centered off Brazil, not Bermuda; the Devil's Sea likewise has no distinctive geomagnetic signature.
holes_addressed: Defender claim 1: 'I never said 180°' — addressed by reframing 180° as our charitable steelman (the only standard referent for 'geometric symmetry' between two surface points), not a dome assertion. Add explicit unfalsifiability pivot: dome's refusal to publish a tolerance turns the claim from confirmable into unfalsifiable.

Defender claim 2: '15.6% is unsourced relative to dome's own thresholds' — addressed by anchoring 15.6% explicitly to the dome's 5.3%-as-confirmation pattern in WIN-029, plus WIN-027 R²=0.7874 and WIN-030 48.6% RMS reduction. Adds a structural argument: 15.6% is ~3× the residual the dome treats as confirmatory in WIN-029.

Defender claim 3: '152° short arc means the zones are roughly opposite' — addressed by computing |152−180| = |208−180| = 28° explicitly, showing arc choice does not change the antipodal deviation. Acknowledges the kernel (the two regions ARE near-opposite in the loose folklore sense) before showing the dome's framework still does not classify 28° as a confirmatory match.

Defender claim 4: 'Their tolerance comparison is hand-waved' — addressed by citing three specific dome WINs (029 5.3%, 027 R²=0.7874, 030 48.6%) with their published numerics, plus the dome's own v22 retrospective on cross-equator residuals.
new_evidence_added: Explicit deviation calculation in longitude: 360° − 152° = 208°; |152−180| = |208−180| = 28°; direction-of-measurement does not change the deviation from 180° longitudinal symmetry. Note added that the great-circle distance (~123°) is not the relevant quantity — the dome's claim invokes longitudinal symmetry, which maps the same in both globe and flat-disc projections.
Anchor to WIN-029 5.3% as confirmation pattern (verbatim cite from raw-text/02-wins.txt line 114: 'H = c/(4x7.83) = 9,572 km, 5.3% from model').
Cross-references to WIN-027 (R²=0.7874) and WIN-030 (48.6% RMS reduction) as additional dome-internal confirmation thresholds.

v22 retrospective from raw-text/02-wins.txt evolution note: 'v22 found circular disc fails on southern/cross-equator distances' — establishes the dome's own retrospective sense of what counts as failure-level error.

anticipated_objections.objection: You're still attributing 180° to me — I never said it.
anticipated_objections.response: We explicitly do not attribute 180° to the dome. We use 180° because it is the only standard mathematical referent for 'geometric symmetry' between two surface points. The dome's published title gives no tolerance, so we evaluate two charitable readings. The quantitative reading uses 180° because nothing else has a definition. The vague reading drops the number entirely — and then becomes unfalsifiable, which is a failure mode in any prediction-tracking framework, including the dome's own (which lists 'falsified' as a possible state).

anticipated_objections.objection: 5.3% in WIN-029 was for Schumann, not for symmetry. Different domain, different tolerance.
anticipated_objections.response: True at the literal level, but the dome publishes a single ledger of CONFIRMED predictions and gives no per-WIN tolerance schedule. If the dome wishes to argue that symmetry claims have a different (looser) tolerance than Schumann claims, it must publish that tolerance. The Mode 3 procedure here is precisely to ask: by what published rule does 15.6% deviation count as CONFIRMED on this WIN? Without an answer, the WIN sits in the unfalsifiable column. Citing WIN-029 establishes that single-digit % is treated as the confirmation bar somewhere in the dome's framework; the burden is on the dome to specify why this WIN gets a >15% allowance.

anticipated_objections.objection: The agonic line locations move over time. Maybe in 1500 they did pass through the regions.
anticipated_objections.response: The Bermuda Triangle and Devil's Sea folklore both date primarily from mid-20th-century media (Sand 1952; Gaddis 1964). Per gufm1 / IGRF reconstructions, the western agonic was near 30°W in 1500 and migrated west to its present 95–100°W; it did not pass through the Bermuda region (~65–75°W) at any of the well-documented 17th–20th century epochs when these zones were named or popularized. The eastern agonic's migration is similarly well-documented and does not place it through the Devil's Sea at the relevant historical epochs. The agonic-line argument fails at every historical anchor point that matters for the folklore origin.

anticipated_objections.objection: You concede the regions are roughly opposite — that IS the symmetry I claimed.
anticipated_objections.response: The concession is that two named folklore zones happen to sit ~152° apart. That is a geographic observation, not a derivation from dome geometry. To convert that observation into a CONFIRMED prediction, the dome needs (a) a quantitative tolerance specifying what 'symmetry' means; (b) a mechanism that explains why these specific 152°-separated zones become disappearance zones; (c) the disappearance phenomenon to be real. (a) is missing. (b) is the agonic-line claim, which fails per IGRF/WMM data. (c) is refuted by NOAA, Lloyd's, and the U.S. Coast Guard. Conceding (a) loosely without (b) or (c) leaves the WIN with no testable content.

kernel_of_truth: The Bermuda Triangle and Devil's Sea really are near-opposite in longitude (152° short-arc separation). 'Notable longitudinal coincidence' between two named folklore zones is a real, observable, geographic regularity. The dome correctly identifies that the two famous 'mystery' zones happen to sit at roughly opposite hemispheres. The argument fails not because the geographic observation is wrong, but because (a) the proposed mechanism (agonic-line correlation) does not hold quantitatively per IGRF/WMM, (b) the disappearance phenomenon itself is refuted by NOAA/Lloyd's/USCG, and (c) the dome's framework does not specify a tolerance, leaving the WIN either unfalsifiable or quantitatively off by 15.6% — ~3× the residual the dome accepts as confirmation in WIN-029. The post-hoc objection (these are pre-existing folklore zones, not predictions derived from dome geometry) closes the door from the methodology side independently.
notes: Patches detail_evidence only. detail_verdict_text retains its post-hoc reasoning; that argument is unaffected by this defense. The TLDR (tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict) does not need to change — it correctly summarizes 'no factual basis for disappearance claim' and 'insurance/NOAA/USCG refute,' which both survive the defender's pivot. The core verdict (Refuted by Data) stands.
